summary: Simon Späti details his comprehensive Obsidian-based note-taking workflow, explaining how he evolved from scattered notes across multiple apps to a unified 'Second Brain' system. He emphasizes using plain text Markdown files for future-proofing, combining PARA and Zettelkasten methodologies for organization, and leveraging Vim motions for efficient navigation. The workflow includes templates for different note types, a tag-based system for note maturity levels, and public sharing via Quartz. His core argument is that this system provides clarity, peace of mind, and the ability to find knowledge intuitively through graph-based connections rather than rigid folder structures.
key_points: Plain text Markdown files ensure notes remain accessible forever, regardless of what happens to Obsidian as a tool
The combination of PARA (folder structure) and Zettelkasten (atomic notes with links) eliminates the cognitive overhead of deciding where to store notes
Vim motions enable mouse-free, effortless navigation and note creation that becomes second nature
Sharing notes publicly creates a feedback loop that accelerates learning and connects you with like-minded people
The graph-based approach allows searching by association (finding a place through remembering a person) rather than rigid categorization
money_quotes: It's not about which tool you use, as you can achieve the same with any other.
Even after Obsidian might die one day, as they are just simple text files with Markdown, they can be opened by any text editor in the past and future.
I do not need to spend a thought on where to store my note as one note can potentially belong to many different areas of my life.

The satisfaction I get from you guys giving me feedback, telling me that it was helpful, pointing out some alternatives or just making friends online, is something you can't replicate in the real world.
This happens more often lately that I do google less, but instead search my second brain as I have written it down as I googled it already more than once.
Never would I have thought, as an organized Swiss person, that I would leave the path of putting everything into folder structures to find easily.
Imagine if everyone would update their articles or notes instead of creating copies repeatedly; the internet would get a web of remarkable, highly valuable notes.
key_insight: A well-designed personal knowledge management system should prioritize finding information through intuitive connections and associations rather than rigid folder hierarchies, using open formats that outlast any single tool.
